scryptogram.solution: i will go and do the things which the lord hath commanded, for i know that the lord giveth no commandments unto the children of men, save he shall prepare a way for them that they may accomplish the thing which he commandeth them.

prompt.referenceText: Then said David to the Philistine, Thou comest to me with a sword, and with a spear, and with a shield: but I come to thee in the name of the Lord of hosts, the God of the armies of Israel, whom thou hast defied. This day will the Lord deliver thee into mine hand; and I will smite thee, and take thine head from thee; and I will give the carcases of the host of the Philistines this day unto the fowls of the air, and to the wild beasts of the earth; that all the earth may know that there is a God in Israel. And all this assembly shall know that the Lord saveth not with sword and spear: for the battle is the Lord's, and he will give you into our hands.
prompt.question: David ran toward Goliath, not away—but his confidence wasn't in his arm; it was in the Lord. What is the difference between trusting in your own strength, and trusting in the Lord while you work? Do you need to change how you're approaching any Goliaths in your life?
prompt.response: "The battle is the Lord's" isn't an instruction to sit still; David still sprinted into the valley with a sling. It's a relocation of where the outcome lives. We can't guarantee the outcome of our trials, but we can do our part with everything we have. Then we can hand the result to God, who always fights for us.

scryptogram.solution: i give unto men weakness that they may be humble; and my grace is sufficient for all men that humble themselves before me; for if they humble themselves before me, and have faith in me, then will i make weak things become strong unto them.

prompt.referenceText: And Saul armed David with his armour, and he put an helmet of brass upon his head; also he armed him with a coat of mail. And David girded his sword upon his armour, and he assayed to go; for he had not proved it. And David said unto Saul, I cannot go with these; for I have not proved them. And David put them off him. And he took his staff in his hand, and chose him five smooth stones out of the brook, and put them in a shepherd's bag which he had, even in a scrip; and his sling was in his hand: and he drew near to the Philistine.
prompt.question: Saul dressed David in the king's own armor, and David took it off—he couldn't fight in the unfamiliar equipment. He went out with a sling and five stones, the ordinary skill he'd practiced for years guarding sheep. What borrowed armor are you trying to wear because it looks like what the moment requires, when we really just need to be our best selves?
prompt.response: We assume the big moment calls for something bigger than ourselves—someone else's methods, someone else's gifts, the impressive armor. But David won with what he'd quietly mastered while no one was watching. God tends to use the thing already in your hand, shaped by years you thought didn't count. Don't despise your own small, proven skill; it may be exactly what He has been preparing.

scryptogram.solution: and adam said, this is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh: she shall be called woman, because she was taken out of man. therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh.

prompt.referenceText: And it came to pass, when he had made an end of speaking unto Saul, that the soul of Jonathan was knit with the soul of David, and Jonathan loved him as his own soul. And Saul took him that day, and would let him go no more home to his father's house. Then Jonathan and David made a covenant, because he loved him as his own soul. And Jonathan stripped himself of the robe that was upon him, and gave it to David, and his garments, even to his sword, and to his bow, and to his girdle.
prompt.question: Jonathan was the king's son, the natural heir—and he loved David, who would take his throne, "as his own soul," and gave him his robe and sword. That is love that hands away its own advantage. Is there someone whose good you could seek even though it costs you something you'd rather keep?
prompt.response: Jonathan had every earthly reason to see David as a rival, and chose instead to love him and arm him for the throne that should have been his own. Most of our loyalties are quietly conditional—we love where it doesn't threaten us. Covenant love is different: it wants the other person to flourish even past our own interest. That kind of love is rare and costly, and it looks the most like Christ.

scryptogram.solution: love your enemies, bless them that curse you, do good to them that hate you, and pray for them which despitefully use you, and persecute you; that ye may be the children of your father which is in heaven.

prompt.referenceText: Behold, this day thine eyes have seen how that the Lord had delivered thee to day into mine hand in the cave: and some bade me kill thee: but mine eye spared thee; and I said, I will not put forth mine hand against my lord; for he is the Lord's anointed. Moreover, my father, see, yea, see the skirt of thy robe in my hand: for in that I cut off the skirt of thy robe, and killed thee not, know thou and see that there is neither evil nor transgression in mine hand, and I have not sinned against thee; yet thou huntest my soul to take it. The Lord judge between me and thee, and the Lord avenge me of thee: but mine hand shall not be upon thee.
prompt.question: Saul had hunted David for years, and now sat unaware at the mouth of the cave—David's enemy, delivered right into his hand. David cut only the hem of his robe, and even that pricked his conscience. When someone who has wronged you is suddenly at your mercy, what do you do with the power to strike back?
prompt.response: David had every justification—Saul wanted him dead, and the chance was perfect. What stopped him wasn't weakness; it was reverence for something he refused to violate, and trust that vindication belonged to God. We rarely get a cleaner shot at the person who hurt us than David got, and the temptation is to call retaliation justice. Restraint of a hand that could strike is one of the strongest things a person can do—and it leaves room for God to be the judge instead of us.

scryptogram.solution: dearly beloved, avenge not yourselves, but rather give place unto wrath: for it is written, vengeance is mine; i will repay, saith the lord. therefore if thine enemy hunger, feed him; if he thirst, give him drink.

prompt.referenceText: The Lord render to every man his righteousness and his faithfulness: for the Lord delivered thee into my hand to day, but I would not stretch forth mine hand against the Lord's anointed.
prompt.question: A second time David had Saul defenseless—asleep in the camp, the spear right there—and a second time he refused, trusting the Lord to "render to every man his righteousness." It's one thing to forgive once; it's another to keep refusing when the chance comes back around. Where are you being asked to leave justice in God's hands rather than your own?
prompt.response: The second test is harder than the first, because by then you can argue you've already been patient enough. David still wouldn't stretch out his hand, because he believed God keeps a more honest ledger than we do. We grab for vindication because we're afraid that if we don't even the score, no one will. Leaving it to the Lord isn't passivity; it's trusting that He sees, He remembers, and He renders to every man what is truly his.

scryptogram.solution: thy servant slew both the lion and the bear: and this uncircumcised philistine shall be as one of them, seeing he hath defied the armies of the living god. the lord that delivered me out of the paw of the lion, and out of the paw of the bear, he will deliver me out of the hand of this philistine.

prompt.referenceText: And David said unto Saul, Thy servant kept his father's sheep, and there came a lion, and a bear, and took a lamb out of the flock: And I went out after him, and smote him, and delivered it out of his mouth: and when he arose against me, I caught him by his beard, and smote him, and slew him. Thy servant slew both the lion and the bear: and this uncircumcised Philistine shall be as one of them, seeing he hath defied the armies of the living God. David said moreover, The Lord that delivered me out of the paw of the lion, and out of the paw of the bear, he will deliver me out of the hand of this Philistine.
prompt.question: Before David ever faced Goliath, he had already fought a lion and a bear alone in the wilderness—no audience, just a shepherd protecting his flock. Those hidden fights were the experience that gave him courage in the valley. What might your current struggles be preparing you for?
prompt.response: David didn't walk into the valley with beginner's nerve; he walked in with a history. The lion and the bear weren't detours from his calling—they were the rehearsal for it, fought when no one was watching. We tend to resent our present difficulties as obstacles to the life we wanted, when they are often the exact preparation for it. Don't waste the bear in front of you; the courage you build in this unseen fight is the courage you'll draw on when the giant finally shows up.

scryptogram.solution: jesus christ himself being the chief corner stone; in whom all the building fitly framed together groweth unto an holy temple in the lord: in whom ye also are builded together for an habitation of god through the spirit.

prompt.referenceText: And thine house and thy kingdom shall be established for ever before thee: thy throne shall be established for ever.
prompt.question: David wanted to build God a house, and God answered by promising to build David one—a throne "established for ever," fulfilled centuries later in Christ, the King whose kingdom has no end. We make our plans for God, and He also building something for us. What is God building for you right now?
prompt.response: David offered to build a temple; God replied with a covenant that reached past David, all the way to Christ on an everlasting throne. We tend to think we're the ones doing something for God, when His work is always the larger frame around ours. God's work and glory is the immortality and eternal life of man. You can help in that work, but don't forget that it's FOR you.
